titleStmt.title: Letter from Andrew Philip Magill to Peter Quinn, 10 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Andrew Philip Magill

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from A.P. Magill (1871-1941) in which he advises Fr. Quinn that a permit has been granted to hold a Nationalist demonstration.The complete correspondence is available here. It begins on 4 August 1916 in a letter from Fr. Quinn Mr. Asquith, Prime Minister. He wrote directly to the P.M. as the new chief secretary had not yet taken up duty. He had applied to the R.I.C. County Inspector for permission to hold a nationalist demonstration but hasn't received a reply and he fears that the application will be refused on the grounds that the demonstration is likely to lead to disturbances. This, he strongly refutes, indicating that the fears of the R.I.C are groundless due to the location chosen. The motivation for the rally is to help keep the local youth from following ‘courses’ other than the campaign for Home Rule.Magill served as Private Secretary to a number of Chief Secretaries for Ireland, to the Reverend Peter Quinn, Parish Priest of Coalisland, County Tyrone.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/5620/13804

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
10th August, 1916. Dear Sir, With reference to your letter of the
4th instant, which has been forwarded to the
Chief Secretary, I am desired by him to state
that he understands directions have already
been given that a permit may be granted for
the holding of the meeting to which you refer
under proper restrictions. Yours faithfully, A.P.M. The Rev. Peter Quinn, P.P., Parochial House, Coalisland, Co. Tyrone.
POST OFFICE TELEGRAPHS
MESSAGE RECEIVED IN THE IRISH OFFICE. Date Aug 9th1916 Handed in at Dublin Castle at . Received here at 5.16pm From Brennan To Magill Your telegram of today re Father Quinn's application
to Prime Minister about Stewartstown meeting. In accordance with Chief Secretarys minute of
yesterday the Inspector General has been informed
that a permit may be granted on condition that
Bands and Banners are eliminated and that there
should be no organised processions to or from the
field and no excursion trains.
AUG 9 16 WIRED AT 12.14pm
Wire Under Secretary, The Revd. P. Quinn
P.P. writes to the P.M. re
proposed meeting at Stewartstown
on 15 inst. As his letter is
dated 4 inst. I wd like to
send him reply today. Will
you kindly suggest reply. The
file is with you. Magill.
Irish Office Parochial House Coalisland Co. Tyrone 4th Aug 1916
To/
The Right â Honourable
Mr. Asquith, Primeminister Right Honourable Sir I beg to inform
you that I have applied to Mr
Miller County Inspector R.I.C. for
County Tyrone for a 'permit' to hold
an openâair Nationalist demonstration
in this parish of Stewartstown on the
15th inst & that though it is a week
since I made this application I have
not yet received any definite reply. I have reasons for believing that the
Police Authorities will most likely
refuse my request on the grounds that
(2) they fear opposition & disturbance from
the Orangemen of the neighbourhood. Now I wish to assure you that their
fears are groundless as we have been
most careful to select for our place
of meeting a locality entirely Catholic
& Nationalist & one in which we have
had large demonstrations before that
there never was the least attempt at
disturbance. Our motives for holding
this demonstration are of the purest
kind, & are mainly to keep our young
men from falling into the hands of leaders
who would commit them to other courses. We the Nationalists of Co. Tyrone feel
very acutely the great Constitutional
Grievance with which we are threatened
by exclusion from the benefits of a Home
Rule Parliament notwithstanding the fact
that we are 55 per cent of the population
& have for years past returned three out of
four members of the County as Home Rulers.
(3) Is it not then more desirable
& safer to have our grievances ven
tilated in the open & thus offer
a safety-valve for our wounded feelings
than to have them find vent in the
halls of some Secret Society? I do not wish to make my appeal
longer & I am very sorry to have to
trouble you amidst your heavy burthens
& cares in these awful times, but as
the new Chief Secretary has not as yet
taken up his duties I felt obliged
to do soâ I remain
Right Honourable Sir
Yours respectfully Peter Quinn P.P.
